sentences.text: Yes.
Charles Fawcett, F-A-W-C-E double T, Charles.
My middle name is Fernley.
Charles Fernley Fawcett.
A few.
[CHUCKLES] In the films, I used Carlo Fizetti quite often, and in the underground, different things.
Bob Wilson, can you imagine such an imaginative name?
It's the first thing I-- and Jose Coembra.
And in the Foreign Legion, I used Jose Coembra.
Charlie.
[LAUGHS] I hate it.
December the 2nd, 1918.
My god, I'm so old, I hate to think about it, I'd have to count it.
Yes.
In Virginia, and it's called Waleska.
I'm not sure the, the address or anything, but it was-- my father was in education, so he traveled a lot and we traveled with him.
He invented a school system that he went around the country inaugurating in different places.
So we moved around a lot.
But our family were all from Virginia originally.
One branch went to Ohio to protest against slavery, and that's where the president of Ohio State University comes, Novice Fawcett.
He's from that family, and--
Yes.
Charles Fernley Fawcett.
Yes.

He was a-- When he went to Greenville, South Carolina to lecture on their school system, I think he was forgiven for being from the North, because Virginia was the North, and Ohio was definitely the North to people from Greenville anyway.
And he was an amateur violinist, quite a good one, and they assigned my mother, who became a mother, to accompany him on the piano.
She also played the harp.
And they, the rehearsals extended quite a while, and he finally asked for her hand in marriage, which was the custom in those days.
And they-- they were married.
And he was forgiven for being from Ohio, because he was from Virginia originally.
Helen Harriot.
Yes.
I had a brother and two sisters.
Oh, yes.
Very much so.
Yes.
My older sister, the oldest is, is Helen Harriot, then my other sister, Mary Isabel, and my brother, Frank Shumate.
He lives in Chicago, where he's in education.
He's a professor.
And my sisters, one sister worked all her life for retarded children, and she still does, and the other one does all kinds of things of that sort.
And they live in Greenville, South Carolina.
No.
Well, my brother, yes, and has a lovely daughter.
My sisters probably thought if all men are like my, like me, they might not get married.
Well.
I went to school in these different places where my father went with his work.
And I was, I was pretty horrible.
My sisters were wonderful.
They were the most-- they were what you call in America the pillars of the community.
I was the exact opposite.
But then I--
Well, then I started playing football, and overnight, I, I, I changed.
In fact, I was voted the most popular boy in school.
I don't know.
I was just, I was just mean.
[CHUCKLES] It took me a while to get over it.
Yes, they were.
They, they, my, my mother died of cancer, and my father of a heart disease.
About five, I think.
About six and-- six and half.
My father never got over my mother.
He was a very sentimental person, and they were both very artistic.
And I barely remember them, of course.
I don't know.
Well, we-- we had an aunt who was very kind to us, and-- and was really like a mother.
And from that point of view, we were fine.
I can't blame an unhappy childhood on my mean disposition in those days.
They, we couldn't have had a better time.
We were very pampered, in fact, by this lovely aunt.
We--
No.
And-- and then we had an uncle who didn't marry either, then another uncle who did and had a nice family.
Well, we stayed a lot in Greenville.
And then, when I was, when I was ready to graduate, I used to-- to make a living, I was wrestling, because I'd-- I left home.
Because my uncle who was supporting us at that time and living somewhere else, but he wrote a letter saying, give up football and-- and be serious or else.
Well, I wouldn't-- no way on Earth I'd give up football, so I moved out that night.
And then, I had various jobs, and I'd had tuberculosis when I was young, so I-- I got a paper route and I would run the entire distance to try and build up my lungs and-- and I started wrestling.
That was another good way.
And by chance, I got into that professionally.
In-- Yes, and then I would use-- That's where I got the first name, Bob Wilson.
I would use that name sometimes so that you couldn't-- if you lost your amateur standing, you couldn't play football in school.
And I had offers for scholarships to universities.

The minute they-- Then one day, stupidly, I let them take a picture of me and they found out I was wrestling professionally, and-- But that was the best thing ever happened to me, when I went off to, to school in Paris instead to study art.
[LAUGHS]
Very much so, yes.
They were-- They were-- As I say, my sisters were the pillars of the community, and very-- we-- I was an Episcopalian.
They were-- They were-- We all went to the same church and so forth.
Yes.
Oh, I went to early communion.
I was one of the-- the only one there one time when it was snowing.
[CHUCKLES]
I even taught school, Sunday school once.
And the teacher said-- the-- the pastor said, speak more about the Bible and less about football.
[CHUCKLES]
Yes.
Oh, yes.
It was very religious.

That's-- They-- I remember so well things like, you are your brother's keeper, and the strong protect the weak, and then we were told-- were told that over breakfast every morning, you know, and things like that.
Yes, I studied under a Jewish sculptor who became my best friend, and--
No, in Greenville.
--and in high school.
And he-- I even-- He had a house up in what we call Paris Mountain.
There's a little here outside of Par-- of, of Greenville.
And I even stayed up there in a cabin for a while so I could study under him.
And he was a wonderful.
He'd escaped from Russia.
About, oh, I guess 14 or 15, some-- 15, I guess.
Yes.
Well, no.
I left there when I was 15.
And then I moved up there to this cabin that someone loaned me up near him.
Just-- But then-- And I commuted to school in a-- a broken down old, what they called a stripped-down Ford.
Oh, yeah.
[CHUCKLES] Without a license or anything.
We didn't--
--know.
No one-- No one knew it.
I know there were, but I remember on-- the football coach, one time, coming back, somebody saying in the back, hey, Giuseppe here is a Jew.
And the man sitting across him, he said, what's a Jew?
And nobody ever thought anything about it, you know?
And we were stupid, as you can imagine because, historically, I mean, if you didn't know, you-- was something wrong with you.
But you know, we just-- we didn't associate our friends, you know, with any particular-- We just were a different religion, but we didn't realize what it was.
Right.
That was one-- That's one of the faults about American education, with-- where children used to be concerned.
You didn't learn, really, about history in the proper way.
Not very.
Not very.
I think they gave up on me a little bit.
[CHUCKLES]
And I was away a lot.
I-- I was-- I-- I went around the world on a tramp steamer one summer and all kinds of things.
Went out to the Dismal Swamp and spent some time with the geeches, and I did a lot of weird things.
I guess they thought I was weird.
I wasn't, but they thought I was.
I suppose--
--I was.
Well, I had a great admiration for this older sister who worked with children, and both sisters were, really.
And then I had a terribly nice brother who was very-- just the opposite of me in every way.
And he's done wonderful things in his life.
I'm very proud of him.
[CHUCKLES]
Yes.
Our football coach, I think.
And-- and certainly this sculpture teacher.
He was-- As I say, he was my best friend, really.
And--
Yes.
I don't know if it ever came up.
I don't know if we ever talked about it.
I remember him telling me how he escaped from Russia and all that.
And then, obviously, he told me about the pogroms and all of that.

And we-- You know, I-- I hate to tell you, but in those days, the curriculum for young students and all was lacking an awful lot in-- in the really important things, especially historical things and things of this sort.
But I knew, somewhat, and I knew that they had been persecuted.
And we-- one time, we had a little run in with a couple of friends of ours about something to do with this, about this particular teacher.
And it was a definite anti-Semitic remark.
But we didn't realize it was quite what it was.
We don't know the significance.
But all I thought was that he was criticizing my friend.
I didn't realize that it was a definite anti-Semitic remark.
Abe Wolf Davidson.
Yeah.
Oh, he was wonderful.
I did in-- later on, when I went-- I went to Washington for a while, and for some reason, I was accepted in a fraternity house.
And I wasn't in school there, I was-- that time, I was wrestling.
And I remember taking a Jewish girl to one of their dances and there were no Jewish members of the fraternity and I never understood why.
I, I'm positive they weren't anti-Semitic, because this girl was the most popular girl, I think, at the dance, and things like that.
I'd-- I don't think we ever ran into that.
Maybe it's because, you know, I would have reacted in such a way, I guess, that, that people wouldn't bring it up with me.
But I never saw much evidence of that.
But I did in Paris--
--later on, as a student.
I think I was about 18, I think.
Well, I studied under-- I studied under a man name, named [INAUDIBLE], and I thought he was Jewish.
When I got to Paris, I started thinking, he's Jewish, and so I must try to help his sisters.
But we found out that the pope had invited them to, to Rome.
They weren't Jewish at all.
This man had painted at the Vatican.
They were of-- they were Polish, but of-- of-- of Swedish descent, [INAUDIBLE].
But I thought it was Jewish, you see?
Right.
So I made a big effort to try and find them, and of course, found this out.
No, while I was in Paris.
Before, I'd studied in-- in Washington under him.
And--
Then some of the ladies I met through him I thought were so terrific, I thought, well, Paris, here I come.
And I decided I'd go there and study sculpture.
About a year and a half before the war started.
About a year before the war started.
In a little dive in the-- on the West Bank, of course, like all bohemians did.
And yeah, it was the happiest time of my life, I have to tell you.
I don't think they knew it.
I wasn't-- wasn't in touch with them.
I-- I lost touch a lot with my family.
And--
I suppose-- Actually, when I went to Washington, I more or less lost touch.
About six months.
And--
No.
And-- No, I don't know.
We didn't correspond, and I have this great feeling for them, but we've never-- We correspond more now than we ever did.
Yes, and then, later on, I saw my brother when I was sent back by the RAF to Canada, and I was in an RAF hospital in Canada for a while with tuberculosis.
And my brother came to see me there.
And I hadn't seen him since childhood, and haven't seen him since.
[CHUCKLES]
No.
You see, I've lived all over the world lately, and I've been-- I haven't been much in America.
No, no.
I went to an art school--
--called the Colarossi, and by chance, I got a scholarship there which helped considerably, I can tell you.
And then I was wrestling, and making, you know, a fairly good living at that.
Oh, yes.
Oh, I did that from early age, and from the day my uncle said, give up football or leave home.
Oh, yeah.
We saw some of it.
Yes.
We saw some of it.
Not so much from the French, actually.
I have to tell you, my experience with the French wasn't-- I don't remember many disagreeable things at all.
They were so nice and so kind to a foreigner, and I never saw any real anti-Semitic feelings there.
But you did see it sometimes among-- and I hate to tell you, but sometimes among American students, not that we saw American students much.
But they stuck together, and I don't remember if they were-- the Jews that I knew were not one, one with that clique, you know what I mean?
The ones I saw were usually fairly successful and already-- and a few students.
Can you describe any anti-Semitism that you saw in Paris?
Yes.
I remember somebody one time leaving the table when somebody came to the-- and making an-- making some-- not an anti-Semitic remark as we would have known it, an anti-Jewish remark, you know?
So I got up and left the table, too.
And then we sat-- this man and I sat-- this young man and I sat at a table so they could watch us, so they could see us.
We sat there and had a wonderful time.
And by luck, two or three of our friends came into this cafe-- it was [NON-ENGLISH]-- two or three of our friends came in at that time, and they came and sat with us instead of with them.
They all knew-- they knew them equally as well as they knew us, but they happened to choose us.
And I was there with this Jewish boy, and having a wonderful time.
And-- and I don't know the other-- the man who made the remark eventually, I think, left.
Yeah.
Not so much, no.
You-- you knew, and in fact, the only people who knew that the war was going to go the way it did, and-- and things were that bad and all were the Jewish community.
And they were telling-- and the writers, the Jewish writers were predicting exactly what was going to happen and all.
I think a lot of them had read 'Mein Kampf.'
Oh, yeah.
I was very-- I couldn't wait every day to get a newspaper, and it became a big issue, you know?
And just before the-- just before the war broke out, it was a big issue, and I-- we were making plans with a number of our friends of what they were going to do.
I was going to-- That's where I got the idea originally of marrying different girls, you know, to get them American papers and all.
And-- but nothing happened then, because everybody kept thinking, oh, maybe it'll be all right.
But believe me, it wasn't all right in a hurry.

Well, of course, we kept hearing-- and then I heard firsthand from-- a couple of things like the night of crystal thing and, you know, there's all the dreadful things that the Jew-- that the were-- Jews were having done to them by the Nazis.
It was well-publicized in various aspects of the press, and completely ignored by others.
But if you-- if you read carefully, you saw practically everything that was happening in Germany.
And there were some very well-informed people there.
And then, by that time, I was-- my friends were mostly a little bit older than I was, and I was striking up some friendships with journalists and people like that, and musicians, of course.
I was crazy about music always.
And so, most of those being Jewish, naturally, I could hear from a lot of them what was happening.
And then you'd run into people like-- people who were leaving, leaving Paris, not leaving Europe.
Then Belgium, I-- I met two or three people in Belgium who were-- couldn't wait to get out, and they were saying exactly, we're going to get out and we're going to get everybody we can out.
And then, before the war started, we knew very, very well that-- that the Jews were going to have one hell of a time.
It was very, very obvious.
It-- it was happening.
I mean it wasn't going to have, it was happening.
And it was obvious.
I mean, you couldn't help but see it.
Oh, that was-- You know, I think everybody has to have something really passionate in their life that they feel very passionately about.
And of course, I have my wife, too.
But that was the thing that really turned me on.
And partly--
Well, I didn't know how.

But partly, the unfairness, the-- the unreasonable, unreasonably unfairness of it was what was so-- Some of the pillars of the-- I mean, the people who were contributing more to-- well, certainly, to art, and music, and all that.
But the-- the people who were real citizens, some of the ones that I would have admired the most happened to have been Jewish.
It was all by accident.
It wasn't any choice or anything like that.
It just happened to be that way.
Vaguely.
Not-- not knowing what to do, and doing anything that-- I had two or three friends who were telling me-- that's how I started finding out about girls.
If you would marry them, they could get papers to go to-- to the consulate.
Had to be careful that, you know, they didn't have communications the way they do today.
But they-- you had to go to a different consulate every time.
Otherwise, there'd be 15 Mrs.
Charles Fawcetts.
Yeah, I had quite a story with that later on.
Yes, that and making-- we made a couple false papers.
I-- I stole a couple of sheets of stationery one time from-- from the consulate, and we wrote in some very strange things about a couple who were supposed to be Americans, and they weren't at all.
And they took it to wherever-- to the-- the, I guess, whoever's in charge of-- of immigration at that time.
And then I think they got papers, you know, more papers, and then those disappeared, of course.
But through that, they got other papers, and the first thing you know, the American consulate in Antwerp really thought that they were Americans.
But it was completely false.
And you know, I felt not one second that I was doing something against my country.
On the contrary, in fact, I think that if we had opened the door much quicker, much, much quicker-- In fact, Mrs.
Roosevelt, who started this organization, was one of the prime movers in getting things started that way.
Emergency Rescue Committee.
She was quite impressed with, I think, Konrad Heiden and a few other people.
And she tried to get someone-- Varian Fry, who, as I was saying, is the most idealistic man I've ever met, he had been in Berlin and he had seen all this.
And he told everybody in America.
He was a-- a professor with a bow tie and silk handkerchief.
Never read a spy novel in his life.
And she couldn't get anybody else to take this job, to go over and-- and try and rescue these people.
And he said, well, all right.
If you can't, I'll try, but I mean, I wouldn't know how to go about it.
But he found out how to go about it, and he did it.
From America by Mrs.
Roosevelt.
Really, by-- by the Emergency Rescue Committee, but she was the-- the force that--
--behind it.
And I think Mr Roosevelt winked his eye to it a little bit, because we were always upset that the government didn't help more.
And I even sent a very facetious telegram one time, long time later, which I probably better not tell, because it was so off.
I-- I was noted for doing a few rather radical things at time, and, but I don't think it helped my standing in the-- in the State Department very much.
In fact, none of us who worked in that organization had very good records at the State Department.
But Mrs.
Shipley happened to have been Polish and she was in charge of the Passport Division.
And all these things came through her thing, and she knew about my friendship with the Poles and all.
And I have a feeling that-- that she sort of softened down some of the-- where I was concerned.
But the State Department wanted us to get out of France.
They did nothing to help us.
In fact, put everything they could in our way.
Oh, no.
Not at all.

No, was much-- I-- I drove an ambulance with the French army, an American volunteer ambulance corps, and when the-- France was overrun, we smuggled some British soldiers and all out of a hospital and took them-- when we ran out, or when were low on gas in-- in the-- in the ambulance, we put them on a freight train and we arrived in Marseilles.
And that's where I found a thing that was going to help us very much with the Jewish question later.

When you went out of the station, Gare Saint Charles, beautiful stairway there and all, you had to pass by the-- the control, and they asked for your papers, and your-- your ticket, which we didn't have, with being on a freight train.
We'd walk down the-- the tracks to get into the station, because the freight yards were some distance away.
And so we saw that they were investigating these papers, and we knew it, actually, but I don't know why we didn't know it before.
So we went in, you  know, one of these bistros there, the-- beside the tracks to-- to get a little courage.
And we saw two-- two waiters looking at us, and we knew we were caught.
We were deciding what we're going to say when they come and all.
And one of them came over and said, oh, you're friends are over in the other-- the other-- in the first class buffet.
And here, come on, I'll show you where they are, and very rude.
And-- so we thought we'd better go, so we got up to go.
And as we passed down the hallway that connected the two, he said, and that door goes over there.
I think out of somewhere-- somewhere, and went on.
And we didn't know what he was talking about.
So I had a friend there, and-- also from the ambulance corps, so we went back and checked it out.
And it was where the servants from the hotel came in to have their breakfast and so forth in the buffet.
So we sneaked out through there.
As we passed out through the hotel lobby, the concierge said, oh, can I help you?
And I said, oh, no.
We're-- we're helping ourselves.
Quite all right.
We went out and found a-- rented a truck, and so on, so on.
You were on your again.
And that's how I got into Marseilles.
